title: Barcode Entropy and Relative Symplectic Cohomology
speaker: Jonghyeon Ahn
affiliation: IBS Center for Geometry and Physics (IBS-CGP)
abstract: In this talk, I will discuss the barcode entropy—the exponential growth rate of the number of not-too-short bars—of the persistence module associated with the relative symplectic cohomology SHM(K) of a Liouville domain K embedded in a symplectic manifold M. The main result establishes a quantitative link between this Floer-theoretic invariant and the dynamics of the Reeb flow on ∂K. More precisely, I will explain that the barcode entropy of the relative symplectic cohomology SHM(K) is bounded above by a constant multiple of the topological entropy of the Reeb flow on the boundary of the domain, where the constant depends on the embedding of K into M.

proof_stages.label: Motivation and relative upper-bound theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates barcode entropy through prior Hamiltonian, Morse/Floer, Liouville, and wrapped settings, then states the main theorem bounding relative symplectic cohomology barcode entropy by a constant times the topological entropy of the boundary Reeb flow.

proof_stages.label: Persistence, barcode entropy, and volume entropy tools
proof_stages.summary: Persistence modules, interval decompositions, bar counts, truncations, and barcode entropy are defined, followed by topological entropy, volume entropy, and Yomdin's theorem comparing volume entropy to topological entropy.

proof_stages.label: Relative symplectic cohomology via Novikov completion
proof_stages.summary: The Novikov field, valuation, completion of modules, cofinal Hamiltonians, weighted Floer complexes, direct limits, and completed cohomology defining relative symplectic cohomology are introduced.

proof_stages.label: Contact-type Hamiltonians and action filtration
proof_stages.summary: For a Liouville domain with contact-type boundary inside the closed symplectic manifold, the speaker sets up collar-shaped semi-admissible Hamiltonians, the asphericity and index-boundedness hypotheses, the Reeb-orbit/Hamiltonian-orbit correspondence, and the action filtration.

proof_stages.label: Action estimates and lower-orbit reduction
proof_stages.summary: Lower collar orbits are shown to have negative action while upper orbits in relevant degrees can be made positive using index boundedness, allowing filtered relative symplectic cohomology to be computed from lower negative orbits of a single sufficiently large Hamiltonian multiple in a controlled action window.

proof_stages.label: Comparison with negative Hamiltonian barcode entropy
proof_stages.summary: The filtered relative symplectic cohomology and negative-action Hamiltonian Floer cohomology persistence modules are defined, and the lower-orbit reduction gives an inequality from relative barcode entropy to negative Hamiltonian barcode entropy for a suitable semi-admissible Hamiltonian.

proof_stages.label: Intersection and Crofton volume estimate
proof_stages.summary: Finite bar counts are bounded by intersections between the diagonal and the Hamiltonian graph on the collar region, then a Lagrangian tomograph of the diagonal and Crofton's inequality convert these intersection counts into a volume bound for the graph.

proof_stages.label: Yomdin bound and collar-slope optimization
proof_stages.summary: The graph-volume growth bound is turned via Yomdin's theorem into a topological entropy bound, the collar Hamiltonian flow is related to the Reeb flow with coefficient equal to the Hamiltonian slope, and minimizing admissible slopes over possible collar lengths yields the final constant depending on the embedding of K in M.

examples.name: Surface Hamiltonian diffeomorphism equality
examples.note: The known surface case, where Hamiltonian barcode entropy equals topological entropy, illustrates the dynamical meaning of barcode entropy before the relative symplectic cohomology theorem is stated.
examples.engagement_reason: The surface case is stated as a consequence where barcode entropy equals topological entropy, with no computation or verification on a surface.

passes.reason: The surface case is used as a tight equality case where barcode entropy equals topological entropy, not as a disproof.
The surface case is presented as a tight equality case for barcode entropy versus topological entropy, not as a refutation.
A surface is the minimal-dimensional symplectic case, and the entropy bounds collapse to a tight equality rather than refuting a claim.

Used as a surface special case where the barcode/topological entropy bounds are tight as an equality, not as a failure case.
the surface case is used as a positive equality/tightness consequence, with surfaces the lowest-dimensional nontrivial symplectic manifolds.
The surface case is cited as a tight equality case where barcode entropy equals topological entropy, not as a failure of a claim.

examples.name: Absolute symplectic cohomology as the constant-one case
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker explains that in the absolute symplectic cohomology case the relative embedding constant becomes 1, recovering the known Liouville-domain bound.
examples.engagement_reason: Absolute symplectic cohomology is only described in the Q&A as the case where the constant becomes one and recovers a known result, without showing the computation.

passes.reason: The absolute theory is the non-relative/base case and attains the normalized constant c=1 in the bound.
Absolute symplectic cohomology is the collapsed no-relative-embedding special case, with the comparison constant attaining the boundary value c=1.
Absolute symplectic cohomology is the non-relative base case of the relative setup, with the comparison constant attaining the special value one.
absolute symplectic cohomology is the endpoint where the relative constant takes its optimal value c=1, used to recover a known result
Absolute symplectic cohomology is invoked as the non-relative/base case of the relative theory, with the embedding constant attaining the normalized value 1.
absolute symplectic cohomology is the collapsed non-relative base case with comparison constant equal to the boundary value one
Absolute symplectic cohomology is invoked as the limiting/no-relative-data case in which the relative constant attains the normalized value 1.
The absolute theory is invoked as the non-relative limiting specialization with the minimal constant c=1, recovering a known bound rather than contradicting it.
absolute symplectic cohomology is presented as the non-relative base specialization where the embedding constant attains the minimal value one.
The absolute theory is used as the base non-relative specialization in which the embedding-dependent constant collapses to the minimal value 1.
absolute symplectic cohomology is presented as the normalized endpoint where the relative constant attains c=1
The absolute theory is the non-relative specialization with the embedding-dependent constant collapsed to the minimal value c=1.

examples.name: Cotangent-bundle/global-fibration Crofton prototype

examples.engagement_reason: The cotangent-bundle/global-fibration Crofton setup is only referenced as prior context for the Crofton/tomograph method, not worked through as an example.

passes.reason: The cotangent-bundle/global-fibration setting is a standard prototype for the Crofton argument, not a sporadic, limiting, optimal, or adversarial example.
